topline: The Supreme Court dismissed Congress leader Meenakshi Natarajan's plea against her Rajya Sabha nomination rejection, acquitted a husband in a landmark cruelty ruling, and the Centre told the court that CBSE's result policy for Gulf students is under review.

stories.headline: Supreme Court dismisses Meenakshi Natarajan's plea challenging rejection of Rajya Sabha nomination
stories.what: The Supreme Court has dismissed a plea filed by Congress leader Meenakshi Natarajan challenging the rejection of her nomination for a Rajya Sabha election. The court found no grounds to intervene in the nomination rejection, leaving the original decision intact. Natarajan herself characterised the outcome as "no setback", signalling the Congress party does not view the ruling as a political blow. The verdict closes the judicial avenue for her Rajya Sabha candidacy in this cycle and underscores the courts' limited role in scrutinising election nomination processes.
stories.lens: The ruling confirms that Rajya Sabha nomination rejections are largely beyond court challenge, setting a precedent that affects how opposition parties can contest such decisions in future elections.
stories.what_happened: India Today, NDTV, and Deccan Herald all report that the Supreme Court dismissed the petition filed by Congress leader Meenakshi Natarajan against the rejection of her nomination for a Rajya Sabha election. The court declined to grant any relief, allowing the nomination rejection to stand. Natarajan, in remarks reported by NDTV, said the outcome was "no setback" for her or the Congress party. The dismissal effectively ends her legal bid to contest this particular Rajya Sabha seat.
stories.context: Rajya Sabha elections are held by elected members of state legislative assemblies voting for upper house seats. Nomination papers for such elections must meet strict procedural requirements set by the Representation of the People Act, and rejection by returning officers on technical or eligibility grounds is not uncommon. Courts have historically been cautious about interfering in the election process once it is under way, guided by constitutional provisions that limit judicial intervention during the conduct of elections. Meenakshi Natarajan is a senior Congress leader and former Lok Sabha MP from Mandsaur in Madhya Pradesh, known as a loyalist of the Gandhi family within the party.
stories.why_it_matters: The Supreme Court's refusal to intervene reinforces the principle that election tribunals and returning officers hold primary authority over nomination validity, not the higher judiciary during the election process. For opposition parties, this makes it harder to use the courts as a quick remedy when nominations are rejected on procedural grounds. It also signals that Congress will need to ensure its candidates meet all nomination requirements meticulously ahead of future Rajya Sabha cycles. The decision has no direct impact on everyday voters but shapes how political parties approach election compliance going forward.
stories.whats_next: Expect Congress to review its candidate vetting process ahead of upcoming Rajya Sabha vacancies to avoid similar rejections. Watch for whether Natarajan pursues alternative routes — such as an election petition after results — or is fielded in a subsequent cycle. The party is likely to communicate internally that nomination paperwork must be airtight before submission.

stories.headline: Not speaking to wife for 13 days is not cruelty, Supreme Court acquits husband in suicide case
stories.what: The Supreme Court has acquitted a husband who was convicted in connection with his wife's suicide, ruling that not speaking to one's spouse for 13 days does not constitute cruelty under Indian law. The bench held that silence in a marital relationship, without more, cannot be treated as abetment of suicide or cruelty as defined under the Indian Penal Code. The judgment draws a legal line between marital discord and criminal cruelty, a distinction that courts have long struggled to define. The ruling has immediate significance for how Section 498A IPC and abetment-of-suicide cases are framed and prosecuted across India.
stories.lens: This Supreme Court ruling directly affects how marital disputes are prosecuted across India, potentially shielding spouses from criminal liability for behaviour that falls short of active cruelty — a distinction that matters to millions of families.
stories.what_happened: The Times of India reports that the Supreme Court acquitted a husband who had been convicted in connection with his wife's death by suicide. The court specifically held that the husband's act of not speaking to his wife for 13 days did not amount to cruelty within the meaning of the law. The bench found that this conduct was insufficient to establish that the husband had abetted his wife's suicide. The acquittal reverses what appears to have been a lower court conviction, restoring the man's legal standing.
stories.context: Section 498A of the Indian Penal Code criminalises cruelty by a husband or his relatives towards a wife, and abetment of suicide under Section 306 IPC carries up to ten years' imprisonment. These provisions were introduced to protect women from domestic abuse and dowry harassment, but courts — including the Supreme Court — have over the years cautioned against their misuse and have progressively sought to define what constitutes cruelty with greater precision. The Supreme Court has previously ruled that cruelty must involve conduct likely to drive a woman to suicide or cause grave injury, not every instance of marital unhappiness. Cases involving wife suicide frequently invoke both sections, making the threshold for conviction a live and contested legal question.
stories.why_it_matters: The ruling refines the legal definition of marital cruelty in India, making it clearer that passive behaviour — such as refusing to communicate — cannot on its own attract criminal liability. This has practical consequences for the large number of Section 498A and abetment-of-suicide cases pending in Indian courts, where the evidence is often centred on relational conduct rather than physical violence. Defence lawyers are likely to cite this judgment in pending cases involving similar facts. At the same time, women's rights advocates may raise concerns that the ruling narrows the scope of protection available to women in abusive marriages where psychological cruelty is the primary harm.
stories.whats_next: Expect defence lawyers in ongoing 498A and abetment-of-suicide trials to immediately invoke this judgment to seek acquittals or bail where the cruelty alleged is behavioural rather than physical. Watch for a potential response from the National Commission for Women or parliamentary debate on whether the legal definition of cruelty needs legislative clarification. The government may also review prosecutorial guidelines in light of this ruling.

stories.headline: Trump examined by 22 specialists, highest for any US President: White House
stories.what: The White House has disclosed that US President Donald Trump was examined by 22 medical specialists during his latest health evaluation, a number described as the highest recorded for any sitting American president. The announcement appears designed to demonstrate the thoroughness of Trump's medical review as questions about the health of senior political leaders remain a global point of scrutiny. No specific health concerns were publicly detailed in the disclosure. The breadth of the examination is notable given that Trump is in his late seventies and serving a second term in the world's most consequential political office.
stories.lens: The health and continuity of the US President directly affects global geopolitics and markets — including India's trade, defence ties, and diplomatic relationships with Washington.
stories.what_happened: NDTV reports that the White House announced US President Donald Trump underwent a medical examination conducted by 22 specialists, which the White House characterised as the highest number of specialists involved in any US presidential health check-up. The disclosure was made publicly by the White House, signalling a deliberate effort at transparency regarding the president's medical status. No serious health concerns were reported as part of this announcement. The White House framing of the examination as record-setting suggests an intent to project an image of comprehensive care and presidential fitness.
stories.context: US presidents are required by convention — though not law — to undergo annual physical examinations, with results typically released publicly to reassure citizens and allies about the commander-in-chief's fitness for office. The tradition of disclosing presidential health details has grown more rigorous since the 1970s, following concerns about undisclosed illnesses affecting presidents such as Franklin Roosevelt and John F. Kennedy. Trump, born in 1946, is one of the oldest individuals to serve as US president. Questions about the physical and cognitive fitness of senior political leaders have been a recurring issue in American politics in recent years, particularly following debates over the health of President Joe Biden during his term.
stories.why_it_matters: The health and stability of the US president has direct implications for American foreign policy, including its posture towards India on trade negotiations, defence agreements, and multilateral forums. Any uncertainty about presidential fitness can affect US dollar stability and global market sentiment, which in turn influences Indian equity markets, the rupee, and export-oriented sectors. For Indian readers, a stable and engaged US presidency matters for the pace of ongoing India-US bilateral deals and Washington's strategic approach to the Indo-Pacific. The record number of specialists also reflects a broader global trend of heightened scrutiny of ageing leaders in major democracies.
stories.whats_next: Watch for the formal release of Trump's medical report, which will be examined closely for any findings that could affect his capacity to serve. Expect political opponents and media to scrutinise the details once published, particularly given the precedent set by debates over Biden's health. Markets and diplomatic observers will monitor any signals that could affect the continuity of US policy commitments.
